abu-huraira: Abu Huraira (RA) was one of the most prolific narrators of hadith in Islamic history, transmitting over 5,000 narrations. A companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), he embraced Islam and devoted himself entirely to the Prophet's company, memorizing his sayings and deeds with extraordinary dedication. He accompanied the Prophet during the later years of his life and prioritized learning over worldly pursuits. His vast memory and tireless narration made him an indispensable source for the Sunnah, and scholars of hadith across generations relied heavily on his transmissions.
aisha-bint-abi-bakr: Aisha (RA), daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was one of the most knowledgeable companions and a major source of hadith. She narrated thousands of traditions, particularly on matters of the Prophet's private life, worship, and character. Scholars and companions regularly consulted her on religious rulings, and she was known for her sharp intellect and willingness to correct misunderstandings. Her narrations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence, especially in areas of family law, purification, and personal conduct.
anas-ibn-malik: Anas ibn Malik (RA) served the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) for ten years from the age of ten until the Prophet's death. A companion from Medina, he was one of the youngest major hadith narrators and lived to an advanced age, allowing him to transmit thousands of narrations to later generations. He is among the seven companions known as the Mukthirun — those who narrated the most hadith. His close personal service to the Prophet gave him unique insight into the Prophet's daily habits, worship, and manners.
ibn-umar: Abdullah ibn Umar (RA), son of the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab, was among the most prolific and scrupulous hadith narrators among the companions. He embraced Islam as a child and participated in major military campaigns. Known for his extreme caution in narrating hadith and his strict adherence to the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail of life, he was widely respected for his piety and precision. His narrations number in the thousands and are found throughout the major hadith collections.
ibn-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was known as the 'Scholar of the Ummah' (Hibr al-Ummah). The Prophet supplicated for him to be granted deep understanding of religion. Though young at the Prophet's death, he dedicated his life to learning from senior companions and became the foremost authority on Quranic tafsir and Islamic knowledge of his generation. He narrated hundreds of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran remain central to classical scholarship.
jabir-ibn-abdallah: Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was an Ansari companion from Medina who narrated over 1,500 hadiths covering worship, transactions, the Prophet's biography, and many other topics. He participated in numerous military expeditions alongside the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and was known for his close companionship and reliability as a narrator. He lived into the era of the Tabi'in and continued teaching and transmitting prophetic knowledge until late in his life, making him one of the most cited companion narrators in the major hadith collections.
abdullah-ibn-masud: Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and one of the most learned companions in Quranic recitation and Islamic jurisprudence. He was so close to the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) that he served as his personal attendant in many matters. The Prophet described him as one whose guidance should be followed. He was a primary teacher in Kufa, and his students became leading scholars of the next generation. His narrations are especially valued for their precision and deep legal insight.
abdullah-ibn-amr: Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who was uniquely permitted by the Prophet to write down hadiths, making him one of the earliest systematic recorders of prophetic traditions. He was known for his intense devotion to worship and learning, and he narrated hundreds of hadiths on a wide range of religious topics. He also had knowledge of earlier scriptures, which gave him a broad perspective on religious matters. His narrations are found in all major hadith collections.
ali-ibn-abi-talib: Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and the fourth Caliph of Islam. He was among the first to embrace Islam and was raised in the Prophet's household, giving him unparalleled access to prophetic knowledge and daily practice. Renowned for his scholarship, piety, and bravery, he narrated numerous hadiths and is considered one of the most authoritative sources on prophetic traditions, particularly in matters of jurisprudence, prayer, and governance.
abu-musa-al-ashari: Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA) was a companion from Yemen who emigrated to Medina and became a devoted follower of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He served as a governor in both Basra and Kufa and was known for his exceptionally beautiful recitation of the Quran, a quality the Prophet himself praised and admired. A prolific narrator, he transmitted hundreds of hadiths and was greatly respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his religious knowledge, upright character, and dedicated service to Islam.
abu-said-al-khudri: Abu Said al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated a large number of hadiths, particularly on matters of purification, prayer, and eschatology. He was among the companions who devoted themselves to transmitting prophetic knowledge after the Prophet's death and was known for his piety and commitment to accuracy. He actively discouraged writing hadiths in his early years but was nonetheless a major oral transmitter. His narrations are considered highly reliable and found extensively in the major collections.
nafi: Nafi was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and freed slave of Abdullah ibn Umar (RA). He is one of the most important links in the chain of hadith transmission, particularly in the famous isnad known as the 'Golden Chain' — Malik from Nafi from Ibn Umar. He served Ibn Umar closely for many years and transmitted a vast body of prophetic narrations on his authority. Imam Malik considered him among the most trustworthy of narrators, and his reports are found throughout the major hadith collections and are held in the highest regard.
sahl-ibn-sad: Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who lived to an exceptional age, reportedly becoming one of the last surviving companions in Medina. His long life allowed him to narrate prophetic traditions to generations of Tabi'in who had not witnessed the Prophet directly, making him a vital link in the chain of transmission. He transmitted narrations covering various aspects of the Prophet's life, worship, and character, and is regarded as a trustworthy and valued narrator.
al-bara-ibn-azib: Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam in his youth and participated in many military expeditions in the early history of Islam. He narrated numerous hadiths on a wide range of topics including prayer, the funeral rites, descriptions of the Prophet, and events from his life. He lived to an old age and continued transmitting prophetic knowledge to the Tabi'in. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.
abusaid-al-khudri: AbuSa'id al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion and prolific narrator of hadith who transmitted hundreds of prophetic traditions across a wide range of religious topics. He was devoted to preserving and spreading the Sunnah after the Prophet Muhammad's (peace be upon him) death and is particularly noted for narrations related to worship, eschatology, and the signs of the Last Day. His dedication to accuracy and his close companionship with the Prophet made him a trusted authority among subsequent scholars of hadith.
ibn-abbas-ra: Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), was the foremost Quranic exegete of his generation and is known by the title 'Scholar of the Ummah.' The Prophet prayed specifically for him to be granted wisdom and deep understanding of the religion. Ibn Abbas narrated hundreds of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran are foundational to classical tafsir literature. He gathered knowledge from senior companions after the Prophet's death, becoming an encyclopedic authority recognized across all schools of Islamic scholarship.
abu-said: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was known for his commitment to transmitting the Prophet's words accurately and completely. His narrations span a wide range of religious topics including prayer, purification, charity, and the afterlife, and are considered authoritative in the hadith sciences. He continued teaching and narrating after the Prophet's death, serving as an important link to the prophetic tradition for subsequent generations.
urwa: Urwa ibn al-Zubayr was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and the son of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him). As the nephew of Aisha (RA), he was one of the most important transmitters of her extensive narrations and a primary source for knowledge of the Prophet's household. Urwa was among the Seven Jurists of Medina, a recognized group of Tabi'in scholars whose scholarly opinions shaped early Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations appear frequently in all major hadith collections and he is regarded as highly reliable.
aishah-ra: Aishah (RA), the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, was one of the greatest scholarly authorities in the early Muslim community. She narrated thousands of hadiths, particularly on matters of the Prophet's personal conduct, worship, and household life. Companions and Tabi'in regularly sought her rulings on religious questions, and she was known for her willingness to correct errors in narration. Her deep understanding of Islamic law and her proximity to the Prophet made her an unparalleled source of authentic prophetic tradition.
abdullah-bin-masud: Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) was one of the earliest and most revered companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was known for his deep knowledge of the Quran and his meticulous care in narrating hadith, refusing to narrate anything he was not fully certain about. The Prophet singled him out as a model to be followed in religious matters. He became the leading teacher in Kufa during the caliphate of Umar and left behind a rich legacy of narrations and students who became foundational scholars of Islamic jurisprudence.
abdullah-bin-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the most learned scholars among the companions, known by the title 'Scholar of the Ummah.' He devoted his life to Quranic scholarship and hadith narration. His narrations and tafsir explanations are central references in Islamic scholarship across all traditions and legal schools. He gathered knowledge diligently from senior companions after the Prophet's death and became an encyclopedic authority whose contributions remain indispensable to Islamic learning.
abu-dharr: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) was one of the earliest Muslims and among the most ascetic and outspoken of the Prophet Muhammad's (peace be upon him) companions. He was known for his strong stance against worldly attachment, accumulation of wealth, and injustice. He narrated numerous hadiths and is particularly remembered for narrations emphasizing simplicity, sincerity, and social justice in Islam. His bold character and proximity to the Prophet made him a distinctive and respected figure, and his narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
hudhaifa-ibn-al-yaman: Hudhaifa ibn al-Yaman (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) uniquely entrusted with knowledge of the signs of trials, tribulations, and future events (fitan). The Prophet shared with him the names of the hypocrites and secrets about events to come, making him a singular source for this type of narration. His hadiths are particularly important in chapters on eschatology and civil strife. He later served as a trusted governor of Mada'in under Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA).
um-salama: Umm Salama (RA), whose full name was Hind bint Abi Umayya, was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was among the early Muslims who emigrated to Abyssinia and later to Medina, enduring great hardship for her faith. Known for her wisdom and sound judgment, she narrated numerous hadiths and was frequently consulted on religious matters by companions and Tabi'in alike. Her narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections and are considered authoritative.
jabir-ra: Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was an Ansari companion from Medina who became one of the most prolific narrators among the companions. He participated in numerous battles alongside the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and spent many years transmitting prophetic knowledge to the Tabi'in. Known for his reliability, sincerity, and dedication to the Sunnah, his narrations cover a wide range of topics including worship, commerce, and the Prophet's Farewell Pilgrimage. He is among the most frequently cited companions in all major hadith literature.
said-bin-jubair: Sa'id ibn Jubayr was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and student of Ibn Abbas (RA). He was renowned for his deep knowledge of Quranic exegesis and Islamic law, and he transmitted many narrations from the companions with great care and accuracy. Known for his courage and piety, he ultimately died as a martyr at the hands of the tyrant al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, refusing to renounce his principles. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as one of the most reliable transmitters of his generation.
abu-bakra: Abu Bakra (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam during the siege of Ta'if and was freed from slavery upon his acceptance of the faith. He settled in Basra after the Prophet's death and became an important source of prophetic traditions for the Muslim community there. He narrated numerous hadiths on various aspects of Islamic practice and belief, and his narrations are cited frequently in the major collections. He was known for his reliability and sincere commitment to the Sunnah.
umm-salamah-ummul-muminin: Umm Salamah (RA) was one of the Mothers of the Believers and a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) of great wisdom and noble character. She was among the earliest Muslims and one of those who emigrated to Abyssinia before the Hijra. She narrated many hadiths and was known for her careful preservation of prophetic teachings, particularly on matters relating to women's religious practice, family life, and the Prophet's private conduct. Her narrations are valued across all major hadith collections.
umar-bin-al-khattab: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam and one of the most influential companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). His conversion to Islam was a turning point for the early Muslim community, and his caliphate is regarded as a model of just governance. Known for his unwavering commitment to the Sunnah, he narrated many hadiths and his juristic opinions are foundational references across all schools of Islamic law. His narrations are found in all the major hadith collections.
masruq: Masruq ibn al-Ajda was a Tabi'i scholar of Kufa who was a devoted student of major companions including Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) and Aisha (RA). He was highly regarded for his knowledge of Islamic law, his scrupulous approach to hadith narration, and his personal piety. He transmitted many narrations from the companions and is considered a reliable and authoritative link in the chains of transmission. Scholars praised him for his precision and integrity, and his narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections.
umar: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam and a companion of the highest rank and distinction. He was among the closest associates of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), whose daughter Hafsa (RA) became a wife of the Prophet. His conversion strengthened the early Muslim community. He narrated hadiths that are foundational to Islamic scholarship and legal thought, and his understanding of the Sunnah deeply influenced the development of Islamic jurisprudence across all subsequent generations of scholars.
sad: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (RA) was one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and was among the earliest Muslims, having embraced Islam as a young man. He was a skilled military commander who led the Muslim forces to victory at the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, opening Persia to Islam. He narrated numerous hadiths covering faith, prayer, and prophetic biography, and is cited in the major hadith collections as a trustworthy companion of the highest distinction.
abu-wail: Abu Wail, whose name was Shaqiq ibn Salama, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Kufa and a dedicated student of major companions including Abdullah ibn Masud (RA) and Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA). He was known for his reliability, piety, and commitment to transmitting prophetic knowledge accurately. He narrated widely from the companions and is cited frequently in the major hadith collections as a trusted and authoritative transmitter. His long life allowed him to serve as an important link between the companions and later scholars.
az-zuhri: Muhammad ibn Shihab al-Zuhri was one of the most important Tabi'i scholars and hadith transmitters in Islamic history. He was among the first to systematically collect and organize hadiths at the encouragement of Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz, playing a foundational role in the formal preservation of the Sunnah. He narrated from numerous companions and Tabi'in and is considered a cornerstone figure in the science of hadith. His narrations appear extensively throughout all major hadith collections and are regarded as among the most reliable.
imran-bin-husain: Imran ibn Husain (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his deep piety, asceticism, and spiritual experiences. He narrated numerous hadiths and was greatly respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his knowledge of prophetic traditions and his closeness to the Prophet. He settled in Basra and became an important religious teacher in that region. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and distinguished narrator.
abu-qatada: Abu Qatada al-Ansari (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from Medina who participated in many military expeditions and was known for his bravery and devotion. He narrated numerous hadiths covering various aspects of Islamic practice, including prayer, ritual purity, and the conduct of warfare. He was particularly active in transmitting prophetic knowledge after the Prophet's death, and his narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.
umar-ibn-al-khattab: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was the second Caliph of Islam, a companion of exceptional rank, and a close advisor to the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). His conversion to Islam significantly strengthened the early Muslim community, and his caliphate became synonymous with justice, piety, and the expansion of the Islamic state. He narrated hadiths that scholars across all generations have relied upon, and his understanding of the Sunnah and his juristic opinions are foundational to classical Islamic legal thought and governance.
al-bara-bin-azib: Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam as a youth in Medina and participated in many military campaigns. He narrated numerous hadiths on topics including prayer, the funeral rites, the Quran, and the Prophet's physical description and conduct. He lived to a considerable age and continued transmitting prophetic knowledge to the scholars of the next generation. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a reliable and sincere narrator.
sahl: Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his long life and his critical role in transmitting prophetic traditions to the Tabi'in. He was reportedly among the last surviving companions in Medina, which made him an essential direct link for scholars seeking authentic narrations from the prophetic era. His reports cover the Prophet's worship, character, and sayings, and are found throughout the major hadith collections where he is regarded as trustworthy.
jabir-bin-abdullah: Jabir ibn Abdullah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions, transmitting over 1,500 hadiths. He accompanied the Prophet on many expeditions and lived long enough to transmit prophetic knowledge to major Tabi'i scholars. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections and cover a wide range of religious topics including worship, transactions, and detailed accounts from the Prophet's life.
urwa-bin-az-zubair: Urwa ibn al-Zubayr was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina, the son of the companion al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (RA), and a nephew of Aisha (RA). He served as one of the primary transmitters of Aisha's narrations and is counted among the Seven Jurists of Medina, a group whose scholarly opinions shaped the foundations of early Islamic jurisprudence. His narrations appear extensively in all major hadith collections and he is regarded as one of the most reliable and authoritative Tabi'i transmitters.
anas-ra: Anas ibn Malik (RA) served the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the age of ten for ten years, giving him intimate and sustained knowledge of the Prophet's daily life and practice. He was one of the most prolific narrators of hadith among the companions and lived to an advanced age, transmitting knowledge to many Tabi'i scholars who came to learn from him. His narrations are a primary source in all major hadith collections, particularly for details of the Prophet's personal conduct, worship, and manners.
al-bara: Al-Bara ibn Azib (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam in Medina and participated in numerous military campaigns in the early years of Islam. He narrated a significant number of hadiths on various topics including prayer, the funeral rites, and events from the Prophet's life and character. He is considered a reliable narrator whose transmissions are well-attested in the major hadith collections, and he continued to transmit prophetic knowledge well into the era of the Tabi'in.
adi-bin-hatim: Adi ibn Hatim al-Ta'i (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam after initially being a Christian. He was the son of Hatim al-Ta'i, famous across Arabia for his generosity. Adi narrated numerous hadiths and lived a long life, continuing to transmit prophetic knowledge to later generations of scholars. He is known for asking the Prophet detailed questions about Islamic practice, and the resulting narrations provide valuable guidance. His reports are found in all the major hadith collections.
muadh-ibn-jabal: Muadh ibn Jabal (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) singled out as the most knowledgeable of the companions in matters of the lawful and the unlawful. The Prophet sent him to Yemen as a teacher and judge, demonstrating great confidence in his scholarship and character. He narrated hadiths on a wide range of topics and is regarded as one of the most learned and reliable companions in both religious knowledge and practical judgment. His narrations are found in all major hadith collections.
ali-bin-abi-talib: Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), raised in his household from a young age and among the very first to accept Islam. He served as the fourth Caliph of Islam and was renowned for his courage, deep piety, and vast Islamic scholarship. He narrated numerous hadiths and his scholarly legacy has deeply influenced Islamic jurisprudence and spirituality across all subsequent generations. His narrations are considered highly authoritative in all major schools of Islamic thought.
usama-bin-zaid: Usama ibn Zaid (RA) was the son of Zaid ibn Haritha (RA), the freed companion, and was deeply beloved by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), who called him 'my beloved, son of my beloved.' He was appointed by the Prophet to lead a military expedition at a young age, demonstrating the Prophet's confidence in him. He narrated several important hadiths and is cited in the major hadith collections. His unique closeness to the Prophet's household gave his narrations a particular significance and authority.
buraydah-ibn-al-hasib: Buraydah ibn al-Hasib al-Aslami (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who led his entire tribe to embrace Islam when he himself converted. He participated in many military expeditions and later settled in Khurasan, where he became an important transmitter of prophetic knowledge in that region. He narrated numerous hadiths covering many aspects of Islamic worship and conduct. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable and trustworthy narrator.
samurah-ibn-jundub: Samurah ibn Jundub (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated a number of prophetic traditions and later served as a governor of Basra. He is known in hadith literature for transmitting several narrations related to Quranic recitation, dream interpretation, and religious observances. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections. Despite some scholarly discussion about a few of his transmissions, he is generally regarded as an accepted narrator from among the companions.
abu-masud: Abu Masud al-Ansari (RA), also known as Uqba ibn Amr al-Badri, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the pledge of Aqabah and was among the early supporters of Islam. He narrated several hadiths on topics including prayer, leadership, and the qualities of a Muslim, and is cited in the major hadith collections. He later settled in Kufa and transmitted prophetic knowledge to the scholars there, where he was respected for his reliability and piety.
hishams-father: This narrator refers to Urwa ibn al-Zubayr, the father of Hisham ibn Urwa, as identified in certain hadith chains where Hisham narrates on his father's authority. Urwa was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina, a nephew of Aisha (RA), and one of the Seven Jurists of Medina. He was an authoritative and highly reliable transmitter of prophetic narrations who played a central role in preserving the knowledge of the Prophet's household. His narrations transmitted through his son Hisham form an important and widely attested chain found throughout the major hadith collections.
amr-b-suhaib: Amr ibn Shu'ayb narrated on the authority of his father Shu'ayb ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA), a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him). This chain — Amr from his father from his grandfather — is a recognized and frequently discussed isnad in hadith criticism, accepted by many scholars as reliable while others examined its continuity carefully. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and contain important legal rulings and prophetic guidance.
mujahid: Mujahid ibn Jabr was a leading Tabi'i scholar and one of the most important authorities on Quranic tafsir. He was a devoted student of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) and reportedly recited the entire Quran to him multiple times, pausing at each verse to ask about its meaning and context. His tafsir narrations are among the most foundational references in classical Quranic scholarship. He also narrated hadiths from companions and is considered a reliable and authoritative transmitter in the chains of hadith.
qatada: Qatada ibn Di'ama was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Basra renowned for his extraordinary memory and expertise in Quranic exegesis and hadith. He narrated from many companions and is considered a major figure in the transmission of both tafsir and prophetic traditions. Despite being blind from birth, he excelled in scholarship and memorization beyond most of his peers. He is regarded as one of the most knowledgeable and reliable narrators of his generation and his narrations appear extensively in the major hadith collections.
jarir: Jarir ibn Abdullah al-Bajali (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam and was known for his noble character and dignified presence. The Prophet reportedly remarked that Jarir resembled the prophets of the Bani Isra'il. He narrated several hadiths preserved in the major collections, covering topics relating to faith, the conduct of a Muslim, and various aspects of Islamic practice. He was respected by the companions and Tabi'in for his sincerity and his commitment to the Sunnah.
qais: Qais ibn Abi Hazim was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from a remarkable number of senior companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), including Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, and many others. He is considered one of the most prolific Tabi'i transmitters from the first generation of companions and is regarded as highly reliable by the scholars of hadith. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections and represent an important bridge between the companions and later scholarship.
zaid-bin-thabit: Zaid ibn Thabit (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who served as one of his primary scribes, recording Quranic revelation and official correspondence. He was entrusted with the monumental task of compiling the Quran into a single volume during the caliphate of Abu Bakr (RA) and later oversaw the preparation of the standardized Uthmanic mushaf. He narrated numerous hadiths and was a leading scholarly authority in Medina on matters of Quranic science, recitation, and Islamic law.
abu-saeed-al-khudri: Abu Sa'eed al-Khudri (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from the Ansar of Medina who narrated hundreds of hadiths. He is particularly known for narrations on eschatology, the signs of the Last Day, and matters of purification and prayer. His dedication to preserving and transmitting the Sunnah made him one of the most cited companions in the hadith literature, and his narrations are found extensively in all major collections where they are regarded as highly authentic.
abu-hurayrah: Abu Hurayrah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and the single most prolific narrator of hadith, with over 5,000 narrations attributed to him. He devoted himself entirely to learning from the Prophet, sacrificing comfort and worldly pursuits to remain in his company. After the Prophet's death, he continued to teach and transmit this knowledge for decades, becoming an essential and irreplaceable source for Islamic tradition. His narrations are foundational across all major hadith collections.
umm-salamah: Umm Salamah (RA) was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was among the earliest Muslims and emigrated twice — first to Abyssinia and then to Medina — enduring hardship for her faith. Known for her wisdom and intelligence, she was frequently consulted on religious matters. She narrated numerous hadiths and continued to be a scholarly authority after the Prophet's death. Her narrations are cited in all major hadith collections and are particularly valued on matters of worship and household conduct.
amr-bin-shuaib: Amr ibn Shu'ayb narrated from his father Shu'ayb ibn Muhammad, who narrated from his grandfather Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA), a companion of the Prophet (peace be upon him) and one of the first to systematically write down hadith. This three-generation chain is accepted by many hadith scholars as reliable, though it has been the subject of scholarly analysis regarding its continuity. Narrations through this chain appear in the major hadith collections and contain valuable religious rulings and prophetic guidance.
abu-huraira-and-zaid-bin-khalid: This entry refers to narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by two companions: Abu Huraira (RA), the most prolific hadith narrator in Islamic history, and Zaid ibn Khalid al-Juhani (RA), an Ansari companion known for his piety, generosity, and devotion. Both were companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), and their joint narrations typically appear in the context of a shared experience or their both hearing the same prophetic statement. Their combined transmission adds strength to the authenticity of the narrations they share.
abu-burda: Abu Burda ibn Abi Musa al-Ashari was a Tabi'i scholar and the son of the companion Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA). He narrated hadiths from his father and from other companions, and is regarded as a reliable transmitter. He served as a judge in Kufa and was respected in the community for his knowledge, integrity, and sound legal judgment. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and provide an important link in the chains of transmission from the companions to subsequent generations of scholars.
maimuna: Maimuna bint al-Harith (RA) was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was known for her piety, generosity, and deep devotion to worship. She narrated a number of hadiths relating to the Prophet's personal conduct, prayer, and purification, offering valuable insight into his private life and religious practice. She was respected among the companions and Tabi'in as a reliable narrator and an authoritative source of knowledge about the Prophet's household.
sahl-bin-sad-as-saidi: Sahl ibn Sa'd al-Sa'idi (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who was among the last surviving companions in Medina. His extraordinary longevity allowed him to transmit prophetic knowledge directly to Tabi'i scholars who had no personal experience of the Prophet's era, making him an invaluable bridge to the prophetic tradition. He narrated hadiths on topics ranging from worship to the Prophet's character and miracles, and his narrations are found in all the major hadith collections.
salims-father: This refers to Abdullah ibn Umar (RA), identified as the father of Salim in certain isnad references. Abdullah ibn Umar was one of the most prolific and scrupulous narrator-companions, known for his extreme care in adhering to the Prophet's Sunnah in every aspect of his life. His narrations transmitted through his son Salim represent one of the most reliable and well-documented chains in hadith literature, appearing throughout the major collections and carrying great weight among scholars of the hadith sciences.
sad-bin-abi-waqqas: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (RA) was one of the earliest Muslims and one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was a military leader of great distinction who commanded the Muslim forces at the pivotal Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, leading to the conquest of Persia. He narrated numerous hadiths and is cited across the major collections. His early acceptance of Islam and his close relationship with the Prophet made him a highly authoritative and respected narrator.
abu-juhaifa: Abu Juhaifa Wahb ibn Abdullah (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who narrated several valuable hadiths. He is particularly well known for a narration describing the physical appearance of the Prophet in vivid detail, which is among the most widely cited descriptions in the hadith literature. He settled in Kufa after the Prophet's death and became an important transmitter of prophetic knowledge to the scholars there. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and are considered reliable.
salim: Salim ibn Abdullah ibn Umar was a prominent Tabi'i scholar and the son of the companion Abdullah ibn Umar (RA). He was recognized as one of the Seven Jurists of Medina, a select group whose scholarly depth and influence shaped the foundations of early Islamic jurisprudence. He transmitted many narrations from his father, one of the most scrupulous companion narrators, and is himself regarded as highly reliable. His narrations appear extensively in the major hadith collections and he is counted among the most authoritative Tabi'i scholars.
al-aswad: Al-Aswad ibn Yazid al-Nakha'i was a prominent Tabi'i scholar of Kufa and a devoted student of major companions including Aisha (RA) and Abdullah ibn Masud (RA). He was a nephew of the companion Alqama ibn Qais and was known for his strict asceticism and deep personal piety. He narrated numerous hadiths and is regarded as a highly reliable transmitter by the scholars of hadith. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is considered an authoritative and trustworthy link in the chains of transmission.
abudharr: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and one of the most principled and outspoken companions of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). He was known for his rejection of worldly wealth, his deep spirituality, and his fearless advocacy for justice regardless of personal consequence. He narrated hadiths on topics ranging from faith and prayer to social ethics, and is regarded as a sincere and reliable narrator. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections and he is revered as a model of conviction.
uqbah-ibn-amir: Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his knowledge of Quranic recitation, Islamic law, and military arts. He served as the governor of Egypt during the caliphate of Muawiyah and was highly respected for his scholarship, piety, and administrative ability. He narrated numerous hadiths covering topics such as prayer, charity, and proper conduct. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections where he is regarded as a trustworthy and reliable narrator.
ikrima: Ikrima was a Tabi'i scholar and the freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), through whom he transmitted a large body of hadiths and tafsir narrations. He was considered one of the most knowledgeable scholars of Quranic exegesis of his generation and traveled widely to transmit and gather knowledge. His narrations appear in all the major hadith collections. Despite some scholarly discussion about his character in early biographical literature, the majority of hadith scholars accepted him as a reliable transmitter of prophetic traditions.
ubada-bin-as-samit: Ubada ibn al-Samit (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and one of the respected leaders of the Ansar. He was among those who took the first and second pledges of Aqabah, demonstrating his early and committed support for Islam. He participated in major military campaigns and later served as a teacher and judge in the Levant. His narrations cover topics including the fundamentals of faith and Islamic practice, and they are found in the major hadith collections.
jubair-bin-mutim: Jubayr ibn Mut'im (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who embraced Islam after initially opposing it. He was known for his expertise in Arab genealogy and his knowledge of religious matters. He narrated a number of hadiths relating to the Quran, prayer, and various aspects of Islamic practice and belief. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections and he is considered a reliable narrator whose transmissions provide valuable insight into the prophetic era.
thawban: Thawban (RA) was a freed slave of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who became so deeply devoted to him that he could not bear to be apart from him. After the Prophet's death, he settled in Syria and continued to transmit prophetic knowledge to subsequent generations. He narrated numerous hadiths and is particularly known for narrations about the latter times, the virtues of certain religious practices, and the importance of steadfastness in worship. His narrations are found throughout the major hadith collections.
imran-ibn-husayn: Imran ibn Husayn (RA) was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) known for his exceptional piety, spiritual experiences, and commitment to transmitting prophetic knowledge. He settled in Basra and became one of its most important religious teachers, guiding the Muslim community there for many years. He narrated numerous hadiths on topics including prayer, worship, and the Prophet's character and conduct. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a reliable and distinguished narrator.
abu-sa-id-al-khudri: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (RA) was an Ansari companion of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) who dedicated himself wholeheartedly to preserving and transmitting the Sunnah after the Prophet's death. He narrated hundreds of hadiths on topics including worship, legal rulings, eschatology, and the signs of the Last Day, and was one of the most active and devoted narrators among the companions. His narrations are found extensively in all the major hadith collections and he is regarded as a highly trustworthy and authoritative narrator of prophetic tradition.
khabbab: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA) was among the earliest Muslims and suffered some of the most severe persecution at the hands of the Quraysh for his refusal to renounce Islam. He was a blacksmith by trade and endured brutal torture, yet remained steadfast in his faith. He narrated a number of hadiths preserved in the major collections, including narrations about the early hardships of the Muslim community and various aspects of Islamic practice. He is revered as a towering example of perseverance and sincerity in the path of faith.
